topic: Locating Africa on the World Stage

study_notes.title: Understanding Landlocked Countries in Africa
study_notes.definition: A landlocked country is a sovereign state that has no direct territorial access to a sea or ocean.
key_facts: Africa has 16 landlocked countries, making it the continent with the highest number of landlocked nations in the world.
An 'enclave' is a special type of landlocked country that is entirely surrounded by the territory of one single country. Example: Lesotho (completely surrounded by South Africa).
Chad is the largest landlocked country in Africa by land area.
Ethiopia is the most populated landlocked country in the world.
South Sudan is Africa's newest landlocked country (gained independence in 2011).
Ethiopia became landlocked in 1993 when its coastal province of Eritrea gained independence.
problems_faced: High transport and transit costs for importing and exporting goods.
Delays in the clearance of cargo at foreign coastal seaports and border posts.
High final prices of imported goods for citizens due to added transportation costs.
Over-reliance on the political stability and infrastructure (roads/railways) of coastal neighbors.
Vulnerability during conflicts: Goods can be easily blocked if there is a war, strike, or political instability in the transit country.
High rates of smuggling across borders to avoid heavy transit taxes, leading to loss of government revenue.
Lack of direct access to marine resources like deep-sea fishing.
solutions_to_problems: Promoting good diplomatic relations and peaceful co-existence with coastal neighbors.
Joining regional economic blocs (like the East African Community - EAC, or COMESA) to negotiate lower tariffs, Single Customs Territories, and free movement of goods.
Establishing 'Dry Ports' (Inland Container Depots) inside the country to clear customs and pay taxes locally, reducing delays at the ocean port.
Developing and maintaining reliable internal transport networks (railways and highways) that connect directly to the coast.
Diversifying trade routes (using more than one seaport) to avoid relying on a single neighbor in case of emergencies.
Using air transport for light, perishable, or highly valuable goods to bypass land borders.

main_seaports_used: The Port of Mombasa in Kenya (accessed via the Northern Corridor road and rail network).
The Port of Dar es Salaam in Tanzania (accessed via the Central Corridor).
ugandas_strategic_position.inland_water_advantage: Uganda uses Lake Victoria as a cheap inland water highway. Wagon ferries transport heavy cargo directly across the lake to the railway heads at Kisumu (Kenya) and Mwanza (Tanzania), reducing road transit costs.
